s: A freight elevator turned museum of the contemporary mundane
w: Exhibition space is a single repurposed freight elevator on Cortlandt Alley — peer through mesh at a rotating cabinet of curiosities: a confiscated ISIS logo USB drive, a failed Flint water-crisis action figure. Founded 2012 by a This American Life producer. The most banal objects as the most revealing documents.
_facts: Opened 2012 by Alex Kalman, a producer at This American Life.
Located in a freight elevator at 4 Cortlandt Alley, Tribeca.
Describes itself as a 'modern natural history museum' in the cabinet of curiosities tradition.
Exhibits have included a confiscated ISIS-branded USB drive and objects from the Flint water crisis.

s: The alley that plays every alley in every New York movie ever shot
w: Crocodile Dundee stalked it. 9 1/2 Weeks was filmed here. Boardwalk Empire used it for Prohibition-era Chicago. Film crews were present three to four days a week as of 2019 — one of the very few NYC alleys that permits production. The cobblestones and fire escapes are the city's perpetual stand-in. You'll recognise it.
_facts: Short alley in Lower Manhattan between Canal Street and White Street.
One of the few NYC alleys that permits film production.
Has appeared in Crocodile Dundee (1986), 9 1/2 Weeks (1986), and the TV series Boardwalk Empire.
Film crews were present 3-4 days per week as of 2019.

s: Brooklyn's attic — a neighbourhood museum of New York City ephemera
w: Started in 2002 as a window display of NYC detritus in a Williamsburg apartment, it grew into a civic institution on Metropolitan Avenue. The permanent collection holds subway tokens, vintage Statue of Liberty souvenirs and fire department relics. A civic organisation as much as a museum.
_facts: Founded 2002 by Dave Herman as a window display of NYC artifacts in his apartment.
Located at 370 Metropolitan Avenue, Williamsburg, Brooklyn.
Not-for-profit community museum dedicated to the history of New York City's five boroughs.
Hosts rotating exhibitions sourced from community collections.

s: A hidden rooftop park over the Financial District few people know exists
w: Take the escalator from the lobby of 55 Water Street and emerge into a half-acre garden at second-story level overlooking the East River. A POPS most Financial District workers have never found. Built 1972, when 55 Water was briefly the world's largest privately owned office building.
_facts: Located atop the plaza of 55 Water Street, completed 1972.
55 Water Street was the world's largest privately owned office building by floor area at completion.
A POPS (Privately Owned Public Space) accessible via an escalator from the building lobby.
Overlooks the East River and Brooklyn Bridge.

s: So popular it inspired Central Park — Brooklyn's accidental first public park
w: Founded 1838 when churchyards were full, it drew 500,000 visitors a year — second only to Niagara Falls, and the precedent that prompted a competition to design Central Park. Boss Tweed, Leonard Bernstein and Jean-Michel Basquiat are all buried here. 478 acres of glacial hills and Gothic gates.
_facts: Founded 1838 as a rural cemetery when NYC churchyards were overcrowded.
Attracted 500,000 visitors per year — described as 'Brooklyn's first public park by default.'
Its popularity inspired the design competition that created Central Park.
Notable burials include Boss Tweed, Leonard Bernstein, Jean-Michel Basquiat.
Made a National Historic Landmark in 2006.

s: A lighthouse said to be built by an asylum patient, on an island full of asylums
w: Built 1872 at the northern tip of what was then Blackwell's Island — a strip housing a smallpox hospital, a lunatic asylum, a prison and an almshouse. Legend says patient John McCarthy built the tower and carved his name into the stone. Named a city landmark 1976.
_facts: Built 1872 by New York City government at the northern tip of Roosevelt Island (then Blackwell's Island).
The island formerly housed a smallpox hospital, insane asylum, prison, and almshouse.
Local legend attributes the construction to an inmate named John McCarthy who inscribed his name in the stone.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1972 and designated a NYC Landmark in 1976.

s: Where Poe wrote his last poems while his wife died in the next room
w: Poe moved his consumptive wife Virginia here in 1846, hoping country air would save her. She died in January 1847. In these two rooms he wrote 'The Bells' and 'Annabel Lee.' The building dates from circa 1797; relocated in 1913. Bronx County Historical Society has kept it as a museum since 1975.
_facts: Poe lived here from 1846 until his death in 1849.
His wife Virginia Clemm died of tuberculosis in the cottage in January 1847.
Poe wrote 'The Bells' and 'Annabel Lee' while living here.
The building was constructed circa 1797 and relocated slightly in 1913.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places; run by the Bronx County Historical Society since 1975.

n: Hall of Fame for Great Americans
s: Stanford White's 630-foot colonnade — first US hall of fame, now half-forgotten
w: Designed by Stanford White, opened 1901 as the first hall of fame in the United States. Its 630-foot colonnade holds 96 bronze busts — Washington, Lincoln, Mark Twain. NYU sold the campus in 1973 and the hall slipped into near-obscurity. The busts gaze over the Harlem River valley, largely unvisited.
_facts: Designed by Stanford White; opened 1901 on the NYU campus at University Heights.
First hall of fame in the United States.
Colonnades measure 630 feet long; contains 96 bronze portrait busts.
NYU sold the campus to Bronx Community College in 1973.

s: The oldest house of worship in New York State — still holds Sunday meeting
w: Built in 1694, oldest extant house of worship in New York State and the second-oldest Quaker meeting house in the United States. The Friends refused to call it a church — the 'meeting house' distinction holds. Sunday meeting for worship continues every week, unbroken through three centuries.
_facts: Built in 1694 at 137-16 Northern Boulevard, Flushing, Queens.
Oldest house of worship in New York State.
Second-oldest Quaker meeting house in the United States.
Declared a National Historic Landmark in 1967 and NYC Landmark in 1970.
Still holds Quaker meetings every Sunday.

s: Manhattan's oldest house — Washington's HQ, then a tavern, said to be haunted
w: Built in 1765 by British officer Roger Morris — the oldest extant house in Manhattan. Washington commandeered it as headquarters in autumn 1776 during the Battle of Harlem Heights; the British moved in until 1783. Eliza Jumel bought it in 1810. Her ghost is said to still walk the stairs.
_facts: Built 1765 by British military officer Roger Morris — oldest extant house in Manhattan.
Used as George Washington's headquarters for one month in late 1776.
Subsequently occupied by British and Hessian officers until 1783.
Purchased by Eliza Jumel in 1810.
National Historic Landmark; NYC-owned and operated as a museum since 1907.

s: The city's first non-sectarian burial ground — invisible from the street
w: Established 1830 in the interior of an East Village block, entered only through an iron gate between 41 and 43 Second Avenue. New York's first non-sectarian burial place; about 2,100 prominent merchants lie in marble-lined underground vaults. You cannot see it from the street.
_facts: Established 1830 as New York City's first non-sectarian burial place.
Entered via a narrow alleyway between 41 and 43 Second Avenue.
Approximately 2,100 burials recorded, mostly prominent 19th-century families.
Not to be confused with the New York City Marble Cemetery one block east, opened a year later.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.

s: Forgotten for 200 years: the largest colonial-era African cemetery in America
w: Discovered in 1991 during excavation for a federal building at 290 Broadway — remains of more than 419 Africans interred in the 17th and 18th centuries. Historians estimate 10,000 to 20,000 total burials. By the Revolutionary War, enslaved Africans were nearly a quarter of New York's population.
_facts: Discovered in 1991 during excavation for a federal building at 290 Broadway.
More than 419 sets of remains recovered from the late 17th and 18th centuries.
Total burial estimate: 10,000-20,000 people, most enslaved.
Enslaved Africans constituted nearly a quarter of NYC's Revolutionary War-era population.
Designated a National Historic Landmark 1993; national monument by President George W. Bush 2006.

s: The oldest Irish saloon in New York — the wishbones above the bar are WWI dead
w: Open since the mid-19th century; no memorabilia removed since 1910. Houdini's handcuffs are on the bar rail. Wishbones hang overhead — left by men shipping out to WWI, to claim on return. The unclaimed are still there. Women barred until 1970. Two ales only: dark or light.
_facts: Opened mid-19th century at 15 East 7th Street, East Village.
No memorabilia removed from the walls since 1910.
Houdini's handcuffs are fixed to the bar rail.
WWI-era wishbones hang above the bar, left by soldiers who never returned.
Women were legally barred until a 1970 court ruling.

s: A 1927 wooden roller coaster that is also a New York City landmark
w: Designed by Vernon Keenan, opened June 26, 1927: 60 mph, 2,640 feet of original timber track. The city planned demolition in the early 1970s; a private operator saved it in 1975. NYC landmark 1988, National Register 1991. Lindbergh reportedly called it more thrilling than the Atlantic crossing.
_facts: Designed by Vernon Keenan; opened June 26, 1927 at Surf Avenue and West 10th Street.
Reaches 60 mph; 2,640 feet of track; maximum height 75 feet.
Planned for demolition in the early 1970s; saved and refurbished in 1975.
Designated a NYC landmark in 1988 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1991.
Charles Lindbergh is said to have called it more thrilling than his transatlantic flight.

s: The ghost station under City Hall — glimpsed from the 6 train as it loops around
w: When New York's first subway opened October 27, 1904, this was its showpiece terminus — Guastavino tile vaults, skylights, stained-glass clerestory, brass chandeliers. Closed December 31, 1945: platform too short for longer trains. The 6 train still loops through; stay on and watch the chandeliers in the dark.
_facts: Opened October 27, 1904 as one of the original 28 stations of the NYC Subway.
Featured Guastavino tile vaulting, skylights, colored glass tilework and brass chandeliers.
Closed December 31, 1945; curved platform could not be extended for longer trains.
The track is still used as a turning loop for the 6 train.
Designated a NYC landmark and listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

s: Oldest Dutch farmhouse in America, marooned in a Brooklyn side street since 1652
w: Pieter Claesen Wyckoff's family moved in around 1652 on land the Dutch purchased from the Lenape circa 1636. Oldest surviving Dutch frame house in America, now in a small park in East Flatbush surrounded by 20th-century housing. National Historic Landmark 1967. New Amsterdam in a residential street.
_facts: The Wyckoff family occupied the site from around 1652.
Land purchased from the Lenape by Dutch West India Company governor Wouter van Twiller circa 1636.
One of the oldest surviving examples of a Dutch frame house in America.
Located at 5816 Clarendon Road in East Flatbush, Brooklyn.
Declared a National Historic Landmark in 1967.

s: A Revolutionary War farmhouse built by enslaved labour, now in Prospect Park
w: Built circa 1783 for Continental Army Lieutenant Pieter Lefferts, replacing his original house burned by the British. Home to both the Lefferts family and the people they enslaved. Moved into Prospect Park in 1918; now a museum that confronts Dutch-era slavery in Kings County directly.
_facts: Built circa 1783 for Continental Army Lieutenant Pieter Lefferts.
Original house burned during the Revolutionary War.
Home to both the Lefferts family and the people they enslaved.
Relocated from Flatbush to Prospect Park in 1918.
Designated a New York City landmark; operated by the Prospect Park Alliance.

s: Where punk was born — 315 Bowery is a boutique now, but pilgrimages continue
w: Country, Bluegrass, Blues: Hilly Kristal's plan in 1973. What he got was Television, Talking Heads, Patti Smith, Ramones and Blondie — and the birthplace of American punk. Closed October 15, 2006, final show by Patti Smith. A fashion boutique now occupies 315 Bowery. The walls remember.
_facts: CBGB opened in 1973 at 315 Bowery by Hilly Kristal.
Birthplace of American punk and new wave: Ramones, Talking Heads, Patti Smith, Blondie, Television all performed here.
Scholars identify it as the linchpin of early American punk culture.
Closed October 15, 2006 with a final Patti Smith performance.
The space is now a John Varvatos clothing boutique.

s: The 1920 Ferris wheel where some cars slide inwards on a winding track
w: Built in 1920: sixteen of the twenty-four gondolas do not stay fixed to the rim but slide along winding rails between hub and outer edge as the wheel turns. Designed by Charles Hermann; Vourderis family has run it since 1983. NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission designation, 1989. 150 feet, still turning.
_facts: Built in 1920; designed by Charles Hermann.
An eccentric Ferris wheel: 16 of 24 gondolas slide along winding rails rather than being fixed to the rim.
Sold to the Vourderis family in 1983 after six decades under the Garms family.
Designated a New York City landmark by the LPC in 1989.
Stands 150 feet tall at Deno's Wonder Wheel Amusement Park.

s: The last American sideshow — fire eaters and blockhead acts on the boardwalk
w: Coney Island USA has kept the ten-in-one sideshow alive since 1985: sword swallowers, fire eaters, contortionists and the human blockhead from spring through autumn. The Coney Island Museum upstairs holds a century of photographs and boardwalk signage. The barker still works the crowd outside.
_facts: Coney Island USA was founded in 1980 to revitalise Coney Island's cultural traditions.
Sideshows by the Seashore has operated continuously since 1985 at 1208 Surf Avenue.
Presents classic sideshow acts including sword swallowers, fire eaters and contortionists.
The Coney Island Museum upstairs holds historical photographs, signage and amusement-park artifacts.
Operates seasonally from spring through autumn.

s: An illegal landfill turned sculpture park, saved from luxury developers in 1998
w: Mark di Suvero created this outdoor park on an abandoned landfill in Astoria in 1986. After 14 years as a temporary park, a developer tried to build luxury apartments on the site; the city made it permanent in 1998. Largest outdoor sculpture space in NYC, free and always open, East River views.
_facts: Created in 1986 by sculptor Mark di Suvero on an abandoned landfill and illegal dumpsite at Gibbs Point, Astoria.
Operated with only temporary city park status for 14 years.
A developer attempted to build luxury apartments on the expired lease; the city designated it permanent in 1998.
Four acres; the largest outdoor space in NYC dedicated to exhibiting sculpture.
Hosts rotating installations and an artist residency programme.

s: Isamu Noguchi's own museum — built in his studio and still there
w: Noguchi converted a Queens industrial building next to his studio into a museum for his own work in 1985. Thirteen galleries and a sculpture garden hold his stone works, architectural models, Martha Graham stage sets, drawings and furniture. Noguchi designed the layout himself. Contemplative and understated.
_facts: Designed by Isamu Noguchi (1904-1988) in his studio building at 32-37 Vernon Boulevard, Long Island City.
Opened in 1985; renovated 2004 to open year-round.
24,000 sq ft across two stories plus a sculpture garden.
Holds sculptures, architectural models, furniture designs and stage sets for Martha Graham.
One block from Socrates Sculpture Park.

s: A free Black community lost for 130 years — rediscovered from a plane in 1968
w: Weeksville was a thriving free Black community from the 1830s with schools, churches and an abolitionist newspaper. Rediscovered in 1968 when historian James Hurley spotted houses at an odd angle to the street grid from a plane — survivors from before the grid. Four Hunterfly Road houses at 158 Buffalo Avenue.
_facts: Weeksville established from the 1830s as one of America's first free Black communities.
Residents published one of the first Black-owned newspapers, the Freedman's Torchlight.
The community sheltered Black New Yorkers during the 1863 Draft Riots.
Rediscovered in 1968 by historian James Hurley using aerial photography.
Four surviving Hunterfly Road houses at 158 Buffalo Avenue preserved as the Weeksville Heritage Center.

s: Manhattan's last farmhouse — Dutch Colonial, 1784, circled by apartment towers
w: William Dyckman built this gambrel-roofed Dutch Colonial house around 1784 to replace the family's original, burned by the British. The family had farmed 250 acres here since the 1660s. Two Dyckman sisters bought it back in 1915 and gave it to New York City as a museum in 1916. Manhattan's last farmhouse.
_facts: Built circa 1784-85 by William Dyckman to replace the family's original farmhouse, burned by British forces.
Dyckman family had farmed here since Jan Dyckman arrived from Westphalia in 1661.
Original estate exceeded 250 acres; this is the only surviving structure.
Purchased by Mary Alice and Fannie Dyckman in 1915, restored and donated to NYC as a museum in 1916.
Oldest remaining farmhouse on Manhattan island.

s: The Victorian folly that became Central Park's official weather station in 1919
w: Calvert Vaux and Jacob Wrey Mould designed this miniature castle in 1867 as a pure viewpoint — no structural function, hence 'folly.' Romanesque-Gothic hybrid in Manhattan schist, perched on Vista Rock above Turtle Pond. In 1919 it became the park's weather station. Belvedere: 'beautiful view' in Italian.
_facts: Designed by Calvert Vaux and Jacob Wrey Mould in 1867 as a decorative folly.
Built in Romanesque-Gothic style from Manhattan schist and granite.
Located on Vista Rock, the second highest point in Central Park, overlooking Turtle Pond.
Became Central Park's official weather station in 1919; National Weather Service data still recorded here.
The name 'belvedere' means 'beautiful view' in Italian.

s: A loft floor buried under 280,000 pounds of soil — and open to the public
w: Walter de Maria filled 3,600 square feet of a SoHo loft to a depth of 22 inches with 280,000 pounds of dark earth in 1977. The piece has been on continuous display ever since, tended by a single caretaker. No other artwork in New York smells like this room. Dia Art Foundation commission; admission free.
_facts: Created 1977 by Walter De Maria at 141 Wooster Street, SoHo.
Consists of 250 cubic yards of earth in 3,600 square feet at a 22-inch depth.
Total weight approximately 280,000 pounds.
Maintained by the Dia Art Foundation; on continuous display since 1977.
One full-time caretaker maintains the installation, removing weeds and controlling moisture.

s: 500 brass rods laid end-to-end across a SoHo floor, adding up to a kilometre
w: Walter de Maria arranged 500 solid brass rods — each two metres long, two inches in diameter — across the floor of a West Broadway storefront in 1979. The rods are laid in five rows of 100; the spacing between each rod increases by 5 mm as the rows recede. Dia Art Foundation commission; on view since 1979.
_facts: Created 1979 by Walter De Maria at 393 West Broadway, SoHo.
500 solid brass rods, each 2 metres long and 2 inches in diameter, in five rows of 100.
The spacing between consecutive rods increases by 5 mm, from 80 mm to 570 mm.
Illuminated by metal-halide stadium lights.
Commissioned and maintained by the Dia Art Foundation; free admission, limited hours.

s: A real 1930s subway station with vintage trains — still underground, never reopened
w: The decommissioned Court Street station, closed 1946, was converted into a museum in 1976. Vintage subway cars from 1907 to the 1960s stand on functioning platforms, complete with period advertising. The station retains its original mosaics and signage. The museum operates entirely below street level.
_facts: Located in the former Court Street subway station, Brooklyn Heights, closed 1946.
Opened as a museum in 1976; now a division of the Metropolitan Transportation Authority.
Collection includes subway cars from 1907, 1916, and every subsequent decade through the 1960s.
Platforms retain original 1930s mosaics and station signage.
A satellite gallery annex operates in Grand Central Terminal.

s: James Renwick Jr's Gothic ruin — a smallpox ward the city walled off and forgot
w: Designed by James Renwick Jr. (architect of St Patrick's Cathedral), opened 1856 as a 100-bed smallpox isolation ward on Blackwell's Island. Abandoned in 1875, declared a New York City landmark in 1975, it stands as a roofless shell at the island's southern tip — the only officially sanctioned ruin in New York.
_facts: Designed by James Renwick Jr.; opened 1856 as a smallpox hospital on Blackwell's Island (now Roosevelt Island).
Renwick also designed St Patrick's Cathedral and Grace Church.
Abandoned circa 1875 when the Riverside Hospital for smallpox patients replaced it.
Designated a New York City landmark in 1975 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
Described as the only officially designated ruin in New York City; remains a roofless Gothic shell.

s: Loew's grandest Wonder Theatre — shuttered for 35 years, now restored to full baroque glory
w: Opened September 7, 1929 as one of five Loew's Wonder Theatres, Kings was the largest and most opulent — French Baroque interior with a soaring ceiling fresco and gilded boxes seating 3,600. Closed 1977, left to decay. Reopened 2015 after a 95-million-dollar restoration. The chandelier never left.
_facts: Designed by Rapp and Rapp; opened September 7, 1929 at 1027 Flatbush Avenue.
One of five Loew's Wonder Theatres in the New York area; the largest at 3,600 seats.
Closed in 1977 after declining attendance; stood vacant for 35 years.
Reopened in 2015 following a 95-million-dollar restoration funded by the city and private investors.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

s: The oldest Dutch Colonial stone house in New York City — hidden in Ridgewood
w: Built in the late 17th century on what was then the Brooklyn-Queens border, this fieldstone farmhouse is the oldest Dutch Colonial stone structure in the five boroughs. The Vander Ende family built it; the Onderdonks owned it for generations. Now a house museum run by the Greater Ridgewood Historical Society.
_facts: Built late 17th century at 1820 Flushing Avenue, Ridgewood, Queens.
Oldest Dutch Colonial stone house in New York City.
Named for two successive families who owned it: the Vander Ende and Onderdonk families.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places and designated a New York City landmark.
Operated as a house museum by the Greater Ridgewood Historical Society.

s: Duke Ellington, Miles Davis and Herman Melville share 400 acres of Bronx hillside
w: Founded 1863, Woodlawn is a National Historic Landmark and one of the great rural cemeteries — 400 acres of glacially sculpted terrain with mausoleums designed by McKim, Mead and White. Duke Ellington, Miles Davis, Irving Berlin, Herman Melville and Jay Gould are all here. Freely walkable.
_facts: Founded 1863 in Woodlawn Heights, The Bronx; designated a National Historic Landmark.
400 acres; over 300,000 interments.
Notable burials include Duke Ellington, Miles Davis, Irving Berlin, Herman Melville, Jay Gould and Fiorello La Guardia.
Features mausoleums designed by prominent architects including McKim, Mead and White.
Free to enter; operates as a cultural institution with guided tours.

s: New York's oldest surviving bridge — built as a Roman aqueduct in 1848
w: Completed in 1848 as part of the Croton Aqueduct, High Bridge carried fresh water across the Harlem River to Manhattan on arches modelled on Roman aqueducts. The city's oldest major bridge: 140 feet above the river, connecting Washington Heights to the Bronx. Closed 1970; reopened as a pedestrian bridge in 2015.
_facts: Completed 1848 as part of the Old Croton Aqueduct system, bringing water from Westchester to Manhattan.
City's oldest major bridge; rises 140 feet above the Harlem River.
Originally built with stone arches modelled on Roman aqueducts; central arches replaced with steel in 1928.
Closed to pedestrians circa 1970 due to vandalism; reopened June 2015 after a 61-million-dollar restoration.
New York City designated landmark and National Register of Historic Places.

s: More Americans died in British prison ships here than in all Revolutionary War battles
w: Over 11,500 American prisoners of war died aboard British prison ships moored off Brooklyn during the Revolution — more than in all battlefield deaths combined. Their bones were gathered by Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge and interred below this 148-foot Doric column in Fort Greene Park, designed by McKim, Mead and White, 1908.
_facts: Commemorates more than 11,500 Americans who died on British prison ships in Wallabout Bay during the Revolution.
More American POW deaths occurred here than total battlefield deaths in the Revolutionary War.
Remains collected and reinterred by Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge; 20,000 mourners attended an 1808 ceremony.
Current monument designed by McKim, Mead and White; bronze entrance doors by Augustus Saint-Gaudens.
148-foot Doric column in Fort Greene Park, dedicated 1908.

s: A volunteer-run archive of radical posters, zines and movement ephemera
w: Founded 2011 at 314 7th Street in Park Slope: an open-stack archive of political and social movement cultural production — protest posters, zines, buttons, banners, films from labor, feminist, anti-war and civil rights movements worldwide. Free to visit; community-run. The collection exceeds 50,000 items.
_facts: Founded 2011 at 314 7th Street, Park Slope, Brooklyn.
Open-stack archive of materials related to social and political movements worldwide.
Holdings include posters, zines, pamphlets, buttons, T-shirts, films and audio recordings.
Entirely volunteer-run; free admission.
Mission: to explore the relationship between cultural production and social movements.

s: A 1950s landfill that broke open and spilled its contents on the beach — still there
w: Named for the horse-rendering factories that operated here from the 1850s. The southern shore became a city landfill capped in the 1930s; the cap broke in the 1950s and the entire contents spilled onto the beach — bottles, shoes, crockery, toys. Time-capsule debris still washes out with every tide. Gateway National Recreation Area.
_facts: Site of horse-rendering glue factories from the 1850s through the 1930s.
Southern portion used as a city landfill capped circa 1930s.
The landfill cap broke in the 1950s; mid-20th-century household debris scattered across the beach.
Part of the Gateway National Recreation Area managed by the National Park Service.
Popular with beachcombers; removing artifacts is prohibited.

s: New York City's first municipal airport — the runways still there, the planes long gone
w: Opened June 26, 1931 as New York City's first municipal airport. Amelia Earhart and Howard Hughes used it for record-setting flights. The Navy took it over in 1941; commercial aviation moved to LaGuardia and Idlewild. Now a National Park Service site with the original Art Deco terminal building and vast empty runways.
_facts: Opened June 26, 1931 as New York City's first municipal airport, on Barren Island in Jamaica Bay.
Named for polar explorer Floyd Bennett, the first person to fly over the North Pole.
Used by Amelia Earhart, Howard Hughes and Wiley Post for record-setting flights in the 1930s.
Transferred to the US Navy in 1941; commercial traffic shifted to LaGuardia and Idlewild.
Now part of Gateway National Recreation Area; Art Deco terminal building survives.

s: Satchmo's house in Corona — exactly as he left it when he died in 1971
w: Louis Armstrong and his wife Lucille lived in this modest brick house at 107th Street from 1943 until Armstrong died here on July 6, 1971. Lucille chose the house over Manhattan penthouses. The interior — kitchen, den, recording studio — is preserved exactly as it was. A National Historic Landmark, open for tours.
_facts: Louis Armstrong lived at 34-56 107th Street, Corona, Queens from 1943 until his death on July 6, 1971.
Armstrong chose the modest Corona house over more prestigious Manhattan addresses.
Lucille Wilson Armstrong bequeathed the house to New York City to create a museum.
Interior preserved as Armstrong left it, including his in-home recording studio.
Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1977.

s: A Victorian water supply reservoir turned secret woodland wetland on the borough boundary
w: Built 1858 to supply Brooklyn with fresh water, the three interconnected basins of Ridgewood Reservoir were decommissioned in 1990. Abandoned, they seeded themselves into dense wetland forest — a rare freshwater wetland in New York City. Sits exactly on the Brooklyn-Queens border in Highland Park. Birders come at dawn.
_facts: Built 1858 as part of the Brooklyn Water Works to supply water to the City of Brooklyn.
Three basins, totalling 34 acres, on the Brooklyn-Queens border within Highland Park.
Decommissioned in 1990; naturally revegetated into freshwater wetland over subsequent decades.
One of the few significant freshwater wetlands within New York City.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

s: The piano family's Italianate villa — they built an entire company town around it
w: Benjamin Pike Jr. built this Italianate villa in 1857. William Steinway bought it in 1870 and proceeded to develop the surrounding area into Steinway Village — worker housing, a bathhouse, a kindergarten and a piano factory. The mansion sat on 70 acres overlooking Long Island Sound. NYC landmark 1967.
_facts: Built 1857-58 by Benjamin Pike Jr., a scientific instrument maker.
Purchased 1870 by William Steinway of Steinway and Sons.
Steinway developed the surrounding area into a planned industrial village for his workers.
Originally sat on a 70-acre estate with views of Long Island Sound.
Designated a New York City landmark in 1967; listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1983.

s: The only 19th-century Manhattan house with its original interior and exterior intact
w: Built 1831 by the hatter Joseph Brewster; purchased by merchant Seabury Tredwell in 1835 for 18,000 dollars. The Tredwell family lived here without significant alteration for nearly a century. The last Tredwell died in 1933; a cousin turned it into a museum in 1936. Everything from the silver to the wallpaper is original.
_facts: Built 1831-32 at 29 East 4th Street; purchased by merchant Seabury Tredwell in 1835.
The Tredwell family occupied the house for nearly 100 years without significant renovation.
Last family member, Gertrude Tredwell, died in 1933.
Converted to a museum by cousin George Chapman in 1936.
Only 19th-century residence in Manhattan with both exterior and interior completely intact.

s: One of America's most polluted waterways — also Brooklyn's fastest-gentrifying address
w: Dug in the 1860s as a cargo canal for coal, lumber and manufactured goods, Gowanus carried industrial waste for over a century. Declared a Superfund site by the EPA in 2010. The flushing tunnel installed in 1911 still runs — the sole mechanism preventing the canal from becoming solid sludge. Luxury apartments now overlook it.
_facts: 1.8-mile canal in Brooklyn; dug in the 1860s for commercial cargo.
Received industrial runoff from nearby factories and combined sewer overflows for over 100 years.
Designated an EPA Superfund site in 2010 due to coal tar and heavy metal contamination.
A flushing tunnel installed in 1911 pumps fresh water in to prevent stagnation; still operational.
Among the most polluted bodies of water in the United States.

s: A 172-acre former military island in New York Harbor — free ferry from Manhattan
w: Originally Lenape land called Pagganck, the island served as a British, then American military installation from 1776 through 2003. Fort Jay dates to 1794; Castle Williams — a circular sandstone fort — to 1811. Opened to the public 2005: 172 acres of car-free parkland eight minutes from Lower Manhattan.
_facts: Known as Pagganck ('nut island') to the Lenape; ceded to the Dutch in 1637.
Fort Jay completed 1794; Castle Williams, a circular sandstone fort, completed 1811.
Served as a US Army post and then a US Coast Guard base until 2003.
Transferred to New York State and City; opened to the public in 2005.
172 acres; roughly 103 acres are 20th-century landfill added to the original island.

s: An 'atmospheric' movie palace designed to make audiences feel they were outdoors under stars
w: John Eberson designed the 1929 Paradise as an 'atmospheric theatre' — the ceiling above 4,000 seats was a simulated Mediterranean sky complete with twinkling stars and drifting clouds. One of five Loew's Wonder Theatres, it held operatic performances during the Depression. The building survives on Grand Concourse, designated a landmark.
_facts: Designed by John Eberson; opened September 7, 1929 at 2403 Grand Concourse, Fordham.
An 'atmospheric theatre': interior designed as an open-air Mediterranean courtyard with a star-projection ceiling.
Capacity of 4,000; one of five Loew's Wonder Theatres in the New York area.
Hosted Metropolitan Opera performances during the 1930s Depression era.
Designated a New York City interior and exterior landmark; currently being converted to apartments.

s: America's first garden city — built by idealists in Queens in 1924
w: Planned 1924-28 by Clarence Stein and Henry Wright for the City Housing Corporation, Sunnyside Gardens was the first attempt to apply Ebenezer Howard's English garden city principles in the United States. Lewis Mumford lived here. Residents shared interior courtyards; private gardens were banned. A National Historic District.
_facts: Built 1924-28 by the City Housing Corporation on a 77-acre site in Sunnyside, Queens.
Designed by architects Clarence Stein and Henry Wright, influenced by the English garden city movement of Ebenezer Howard.
First built community of the Regional Planning Association of America (RPAA).
Lewis Mumford, Clarence Stein and other RPAA founders lived here.
Designated a National Historic District.

n: Panorama of the City of New York
s: A 1:1200 scale model of every building in New York — all 895,000 of them
w: Robert Moses commissioned Robert Gould to build a complete scale model of New York City for the 1964 World's Fair: 895,000 individual buildings across 9,335 square feet of floor space at 1:1200 scale. Planes that once flew overhead have been removed; the model is periodically updated. At the Queens Museum since 1972.
_facts: Commissioned by Robert Moses for the 1964-65 New York World's Fair.
Built by Robert Gould and a team of 100 workers over three years.
Scale 1:1200; 9,335 square feet; represents all five boroughs with 895,000 individual building models.
Originally included radio-controlled model planes flying over it; these were removed.
Has been at the Queens Museum (in Flushing Meadows Corona Park) since 1972; regularly updated to reflect new construction.

s: A Greek Revival manor in the Bronx with terraced gardens overlooking Long Island Sound
w: Built circa 1842 on land the Pell family acquired in 1654 from Native Americans, this Greek Revival mansion is the last surviving manor house in Pelham Bay Park. Bought by the city in 1888 and used as a mayoral summer residence, it was leased to the International Garden Club in 1914 and opened as a museum in 1946. Remote and quietly extraordinary.
_facts: Built circa 1842; the Pell family purchased the surrounding land in 1654.
Greek Revival exterior; Federal interiors; last surviving manor house in the Pelham Bay Park area.
New York City acquired the property in 1888; used as a summer residence by several mayors.
International Garden Club leased it in 1914 and restored the terraced gardens.
Opened as a museum in 1946; designated a New York City landmark.

s: Dylan Thomas drank his last here. Now buy one yourself.
w: Opened 1880 as a longshoremen's bar; became the nerve centre of Greenwich Village bohemia in the 1950s when Dylan Thomas and his circle arrived. Thomas returned to the Chelsea Hotel after a drinking session here on November 4, 1953 and died days later. Kerouac was banned. James Baldwin, Bob Dylan, Jim Morrison and Norman Mailer all drank at the same battered tables.
_facts: Bar opened 1880 at 567 Hudson Street, West Village; originally a longshoremen's bar.
Became a literary landmark in the early 1950s when Dylan Thomas began frequenting it.
Dylan Thomas was drinking here shortly before his death on November 9, 1953.
Other notable patrons include Jack Kerouac (who was banned), James Baldwin, Bob Dylan, Jim Morrison and Norman Mailer.
One of the oldest continuously operating bars in New York City.

s: The Harlem brownstone where a poet rewrote America.
w: Langston Hughes occupied the top floor of this 1869 Italianate rowhouse at 20 East 127th Street from 1947 until his death in 1967 — the longest settled address of his life. Here he wrote 'Montage of a Dream Deferred' and 'I Wonder as I Wander.' The building's owners were his adopted aunt and uncle. NYC landmark since 1981; a restoration is underway.
_facts: Hughes lived and worked here from 1947 until his death in 1967.
Address: 20 East 127th Street, East Harlem, Manhattan.
He wrote 'Montage of a Dream Deferred' (1951) and 'I Wonder as I Wander' (1956) in this house.
The 1869 Italianate brownstone belonged to Hughes's adopted uncle and aunt, Emerson and Ethel Harper.
Designated a NYC Landmark 1981; listed on the National Register of Historic Places 1982.

s: The cramped Harlem room where bebop was invented.
w: Henry Minton opened his club in the Cecil Hotel in 1938 and instituted weekly jam sessions that drew the city's best jazz musicians. Thelonious Monk, Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Charlie Christian and Kenny Clarke worked out the harmonic vocabulary of bebop here in the early 1940s. Closed 1974; reopened 2013. National Register of Historic Places.
_facts: Founded 1938 by Henry Minton, the first Black delegate to the American Federation of Musicians Local 802.
Located at 206-210 West 118th Street in the Cecil Hotel, Harlem.
Thelonious Monk, Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Charlie Christian and Bud Powell pioneered bebop here in jam sessions circa 1941-44.
Closed in 1974; reopened under its original name in 2013.
Designated a New York City landmark 2023; listed on the National Register of Historic Places 1985.

s: A Bronx rec room. The night of August 11, 1973.
w: DJ Kool Herc threw a back-to-school party in the community room of this 18-storey apartment building on August 11, 1973 — isolating the percussion break between two records and looping it for the dancers. That technique, the 'breakbeat,' became the sonic foundation of hip-hop. Herc later called it 'the Bethlehem of hip-hop culture.'
_facts: 102-unit residential building at 1520 Sedgwick Avenue, Morris Heights, The Bronx, completed 1967.
DJ Kool Herc (Clive Campbell) held a back-to-school party in the recreation room on August 11, 1973.
Herc's technique of isolating and looping drum breaks is considered the founding moment of hip-hop.
Herc described 1520 Sedgwick as 'the Bethlehem of hip-hop culture' during a rally to save the building.
The New York Times has reported it as the historically accepted birthplace of hip-hop.

s: Cappuccino in a 1902 machine, since 1927.
w: Domenico Parisi opened this MacDougal Street cafe in 1927 and brought the first cappuccino machine to the United States — an 1902 original that still stands against the back wall. The bench in the corner came from a Medici palazzo in Florence. Filmed in The Godfather Part II, Serpico and Inside Llewyn Davis. JFK gave a speech outside in 1959.
_facts: Opened 1927 at 119 MacDougal Street, Greenwich Village, by Domenico Parisi.
Parisi introduced cappuccino to America; the original 1902 espresso machine remains in the cafe.
A bench from a Florentine Medici palazzo sits in the interior, alongside a painting from a follower of Caravaggio.
Appeared in The Godfather Part II (1974), Serpico (1973), Inside Llewyn Davis (2013) and others.
John F. Kennedy made a campaign speech outside the cafe in 1959.

s: Oldest bialy bakery in America, still firing at dawn.
w: The Bialystoker Kuchen Bakery has been baking bialys on Grand Street since 1936 — the doughy, onion-pocked cousin of the bagel brought from Bialystok by Jewish immigrants. The bakery fires up in the small hours; the first trays come out before dawn. Oldest bialy operation in the United States.
_facts: Kossar's Bialystoker Kuchen Bakery established 1936 at 367 Grand Street, Lower East Side.
The bialy originates from Bialystok, Poland; the name 'bialy' is short for bialystoker kuchen ('Bialystok cake').
Oldest bialy bakery in the United States.
Baking takes place overnight; first bialys are ready before dawn.
The shop has survived multiple ownership changes while retaining traditional methods.

s: Keith Haring graffitied it in 1982. It keeps changing.
w: The blank concrete wall at the corner of Houston and the Bowery has been New York's most prominent outdoor mural surface since Keith Haring painted it in 1982. Goldman Properties gave curator Jeffrey Deitch control of the commissions in 2008; Shepard Fairey, Os Gemeos, Banksy and others have each left their mark. The work rotates every six to twelve months.
_facts: Concrete wall at Houston Street and the Bowery, East Village / NoHo, owned by Goldman Properties.
Keith Haring created a large mural on the wall in 1982, establishing it as a graffiti landmark.
Goldman Properties began commissioning large-scale murals in 2008 via curator Jeffrey Deitch.
Artists who have shown work on the wall include Shepard Fairey, Os Gemeos, Kenny Scharf, FAILE and Banksy.
Banksy used the wall in 2018 to protest the imprisonment of a Turkish artist.

s: The graffiti mecca they whitewashed overnight.
w: From the early 1990s to 2013, the former Neptune Meter Company factory at 45-46 Davis Street in Long Island City was covered with permission murals by graffiti artists from around the world — the largest aerosol art site in North America. In November 2013 the developer whitewashed all the walls overnight before a court injunction could protect them. Demolished 2014; luxury towers stand in its place.
_facts: The 5 Pointz building at 45-46 Davis Street, Long Island City was an 1892 factory (Neptune Meter Company).
From the early 1990s the exterior was used as a legal mural space; artist Jonathan 'Meres One' Cohen became curator.
The site was the largest open-air aerosol art exhibition space in North America.
In November 2013, developer Jerry Wolkoff had all works whitewashed overnight before courts could intervene.
A federal jury awarded 6.75 million dollars in damages to 49 artists under the Visual Artists Rights Act in 2018.

s: The Marx Brothers shot their first two films here — Queens, not Hollywood.
w: Famous Players-Lasky built this studio complex in Astoria in 1920 — close to Broadway's talent, away from California's wildfires. The Marx Brothers made 'The Cocoanuts' (1929) and 'Animal Crackers' (1930) here. W. C. Fields shot silents on the same stages. Paramount left for Hollywood in 1932; the site survived, became Kaufman Astoria Studios in 1982, and is now NYC's only working backlot.
_facts: Studio constructed 1920-21 for Famous Players-Lasky (later Paramount Pictures) at 35th Avenue, Astoria, Queens.
Marx Brothers filmed 'The Cocoanuts' (1929) and 'Animal Crackers' (1930) here — their first two sound films.
W. C. Fields made several silent-era features at the Astoria studio.
Paramount vacated in 1932 as all operations moved to California.
Designated a National Historic District 1978; renamed Kaufman Astoria Studios by developer George Kaufman in 1982.

s: Rosemary had her baby here. So did the 1880s.
w: Built 1880-84 as one of New York's first luxury apartment buildings — so remote the joke was it might as well be in Dakota Territory. Roman Polanski filmed 'Rosemary's Baby' on its facade and in the courtyard in 1968. John Lennon lived here from 1973; he was shot in the archway on December 8, 1980. Strawberry Fields is across the street.
_facts: Built 1880-84 at 1 West 72nd Street by developer Edward Cabot Clark; designed by Henry Janeway Hardenbergh.
Name reportedly comes from the building's remote location on the Upper West Side, joked to be 'as far as Dakota Territory.'
Roman Polanski used the facade and courtyard for 'Rosemary's Baby' (1968).
John Lennon and Yoko Ono lived here from 1973; Lennon was shot in the archway on December 8, 1980.
National Historic Landmark; designated a New York City landmark 1969.

s: The first great synagogue built by Eastern European Jews in America.
w: Congregation Kahal Adath Jeshurun dedicated this Moorish Revival building on September 4, 1887 — the grandest synagogue yet erected by Eastern European Jewish immigrants, who had previously prayed in tenement basements. Up to 800 members by 1915; the main sanctuary was closed in 1954 as the congregation dwindled. A 20-year restoration completed 2007. Services still held weekly.
_facts: Built 1887 at 12-16 Eldridge Street for Congregation Kahal Adath Jeshurun — one of the first major synagogues built by Eastern European Jews in the US.
Moorish Revival design by architects Peter and Francis William Herter; construction cost 91,907 dollars.
Membership peaked at around 800 between 1890 and 1915.
Main sanctuary closed 1954; congregation met in the basement; restoration completed 2007.
National Historic Landmark; designated NYC landmark 1980.

s: The only Romaniote synagogue in the Western Hemisphere.
w: The Greek Jewish congregation of Janina built this Classical Revival synagogue on Broome Street between 1925 and 1927. Romaniote Jews — the oldest Jewish community in Europe, rooted in the Eastern Mediterranean for nearly 2,000 years before the Spanish Inquisition — have a distinct rite separate from both Sephardic and Ashkenazi Judaism. This is the only synagogue of that tradition in the Western Hemisphere.
_facts: Built 1925-27 at 280 Broome Street, Lower East Side, by the Romaniote Jewish community from Janina, Greece.
Only Romaniote-rite synagogue in the Western Hemisphere.
Romaniote Judaism is distinct from both Sephardic and Ashkenazi traditions; the community traces its Aegean roots back nearly 2,000 years.
Designed by Sydney Daub in Classical Revival and Moorish Revival styles.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places 1999; designated NYC Landmark 2004.

n: Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture
s: An Afro-Puerto Rican collector who built a world archive.
w: Arturo Alfonso Schomburg was born in Puerto Rico in 1874 and spent his life amassing manuscripts, art and artifacts documenting the African diaspora — driven by a teacher who told him Black people had no history. He donated his collection to the New York Public Library in 1926; it became the cornerstone of this Harlem research centre. Five specialist divisions; open to the public.
_facts: Located at 515 Malcolm X Boulevard (Lenox Avenue), Harlem; part of the New York Public Library system.
Named for Arturo Alfonso Schomburg (1874-1938), an Afro-Puerto Rican bibliophile and historian.
Schomburg donated his personal collection to the NYPL in 1926; it became the nucleus of the center.
Holds five divisions: Art and Artifacts; General Research and Reference; Manuscripts, Archives and Rare Books; Moving Image and Recorded Sound; Photographs and Prints.
Open to the general public; hosts readings, exhibitions and theatrical events.

s: 50 folding chairs, one projector, and a revolution.
w: Film Forum started in 1970 in a loft with 50 folding chairs, one projector and a 19,000-dollar annual budget. It has shown every major retrospective in American cinema since. The only autonomous nonprofit cinema in New York City, it moved to its current Houston Street home in 1990. Karen Cooper has been director since 1972.
_facts: Founded 1970 as an alternative screening space with 50 folding chairs, one projector and a 19,000-dollar budget.
Located since 1990 at 209 West Houston Street, Greenwich Village.
The only autonomous nonprofit cinema in New York City and one of the few in the United States.
Karen Cooper became director in 1972.
Up to 250,000 admissions per year; over 6,500 members; open 365 days a year.

s: Industrial gantries, a Pepsi sign, and Manhattan framed in steel.
w: A former dockyard on the Hunters Point waterfront where car-float transfer bridges loaded freight railcars onto barges bound for Manhattan. The four iron gantries — built in 1925 for the Long Island Rail Road — are the park's centrepiece. The northern section was a PepsiCo bottling plant until 1999; its rooftop Pepsi-Cola sign became a Queens landmark before the park absorbed it.
_facts: 12-acre state park at Hunters Point, Long Island City; opened May 1998.
Former dockyard with car-float transfer bridges built in 1925 to serve the LIRR's North Shore Freight Branch.
The 'contained apron' transfer bridges are of the James B. French patent (1910).
Northern section was a PepsiCo bottling plant until 1999; the Pepsi-Cola rooftop sign is preserved in the park.
Park faces Midtown Manhattan across the East River.

s: Six floors of rare books. Jackie Kennedy shopped here.
w: Louis Cohen founded Argosy in 1925 on Book Row and named it with an 'A' so it would appear early in phone directories. A six-storey Midtown townhouse now holds first editions, Americana, antique maps, autographs and bargain books. Franklin Roosevelt ordered catalogues. Jackie Kennedy had the store stock the White House library with Americana. Third-generation family ownership.
_facts: Founded 1925 by Louis Cohen; originally located on Book Row (Fourth Avenue); moved to 116 East 59th Street in the 1960s.
Name chosen partly because beginning with 'A' would place it early in telephone directories.
NYC's oldest independent bookstore; occupies a full six-story townhouse.
President Franklin D. Roosevelt was a catalogue customer; Jacqueline Kennedy used the store to stock the White House library.
Now operated by the third generation of the Cohen family.

s: Decommissioned 1995. Pumped water on 9/11.
w: Built in 1931, the John J. Harvey was the most powerful fireboat in the world at launch — capable of pumping 18,000 gallons per minute. Retired by the FDNY in 1995 and bought by a group of volunteers who restored it as a museum vessel. On September 11, 2001, the city called it back into service to pump water to Ground Zero for 80 hours after the water mains burst.
_facts: Built 1931; served the FDNY until decommissioned in 1995.
At launch, one of the most powerful fireboats ever built; capable of pumping 18,000 gallons of water per minute.
Purchased by volunteers and preserved as a museum vessel moored at Pier 66a, Hudson River Park.
Recalled to active service on September 11, 2001 to pump river water to Ground Zero after water mains failed.
Operated for approximately 80 hours during the 9/11 emergency response.

s: O. Henry wrote The Gift of the Magi in that booth. Probably.
w: The building dates to 1829; liquor has been served continuously here since at least 1864 — making this the oldest surviving continuously operating bar in New York City. O. Henry lived two blocks away from 1903 to 1907 and set one story here under the name 'Kenealy's.' Local legend says he wrote 'The Gift of the Magi' in the second booth from the front. Historians are sceptical. The booth is still there.
_facts: Building dates to 1829 at 129 East 18th Street (corner of Irving Place), Gramercy Park.
Liquor sold here from at least 1864; oldest continuously operating bar and restaurant in New York City.
O. Henry lived at 55 Irving Place (two blocks away) 1903-07; he set his story 'The Lost Blend' in the bar as 'Kenealy's.'
Legend that he wrote 'The Gift of the Magi' in the second front booth is noted as apocryphal by the American Institute of Architects.
During Prohibition, the bar operated disguised as a flower shop.

s: Burroughs, Twain, Sid Vicious: every floor a scandal.
w: Built 1883-84 as a housing cooperative; converted to a hotel in 1905. Thomas Wolfe wrote 'You Can't Go Home Again' here. Arthur C. Clarke called it his 'spiritual home' while writing '2001: A Space Odyssey.' Dylan Thomas was carried out unconscious after a drinking session in 1953 and died days later. Sid Vicious stabbed Nancy Spungen in Room 100 in 1978. Now a luxury hotel; the red iron balconies remain.
_facts: Built 1883-84 at 222 West 23rd Street by the Chelsea Association; designed by Philip Hubert in Queen Anne Revival / Victorian Gothic style.
Thomas Wolfe lived here and wrote 'You Can't Go Home Again' before his death in 1938.
Arthur C. Clarke wrote '2001: A Space Odyssey' while staying at the Chelsea, calling it his 'spiritual home.'
Dylan Thomas was brought here from the White Horse Tavern in November 1953 and died shortly after.
Sid Vicious's girlfriend Nancy Spungen was found stabbed to death in Room 100 in October 1978.

s: Brooklyn hill with the best secret skyline view.
w: Between 41st and 44th Streets on a ridge above the harbor, this 24-acre park offers one of the highest free viewpoints in Brooklyn — southwest towards the Narrows and Verrazano Bridge, northeast across lower Manhattan. Named for its sunsets, which are best in autumn. The park has been the centre of Brooklyn's Scandinavian then Chinese and Latino communities since the 1890s.
_facts: 24.5-acre park between Fifth and Seventh Avenues, 41st to 44th Streets, Sunset Park, Brooklyn.
Sits on a glacial ridge; one of the highest natural elevations in western Brooklyn.
Offers panoramic views of New York Harbor, the Verrazano-Narrows Bridge, and Lower Manhattan.
Neighbourhood was largely Scandinavian in the early 20th century; now predominantly Chinese and Latino.
Open since the 1890s; named for its harbour sunsets.
